title: Connected Funds
abstract: Mutual funds often invest in other funds. In this paper, we analyze the economics behind such cross-fund investments and investigate their financial stability implications. Using granular data for the German fund sector, our main findings are that cross-fund investments (a) are becoming increasingly important over time, (b) were heavily liquidated during March 2020, and (c) display measurable contagion effects. Overall, cross-fund investments can elevate structural fund sector vulnerabilities.

article.1. Data and Institutional Details:
JEL G10 - General G11 - Portfolio Choice; Investment Decisions G23 - Non-bank Financial Institutions; Financial Instruments; Institutional Investors Editor: Itay Goldstein Itay Goldstein Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

The connectedness of the financial system can be a source of financial instability. Ever since the global financial crisis of 2007‚Äì2008, much research has been devoted to systemic risk among banks (Glasserman and Young 2016), but over recent years the focus has shifted toward nonbank financial intermediaries. In this regard, open-ended investment funds have received particular attention both because of their strong growth and because of strategic complementarities that give rise to liquidity/run risks (Goldstein, Jiang, and Ng 2017). A growing literature explores structural fund sector vulnerabilities from common asset holdings in the form of fire sale externalities, and substantial evidence points to funds‚Äô fire sales having persistent price effects (Coval and Stafford 2007;Manconi, Massa, and Yasuda 2012;Ant√≥n and Polk 2014;Chernenko and Sunderam 2020).

In this paper, we highlight another potentially important source of fund sector vulnerabilities that has been overlooked by the academic literature, namely, that funds increasingly invest in other funds.Figure 1illustrates that such direct fund-to-fund connections (orcross-fund holdings) increased quite dramatically over recent years in many jurisdictions. For example, the figure shows that the aggregate share of fund shares in German funds‚Äô asset portfolios has increased from 10%to more than 20%since 2009. A comparable increase can be observed for the United States, with recent estimates suggesting that roughly 50%of all registered U.S. funds invest in other funds (Securities and Exchange Commission 2018).

Since the ICI U.S. mutual fund totals (table 1 of the Fact Book) exclude funds that primarily invest in other funds, we show the TNA of funds of funds (table 48 of the Fact Book) as a share of the TNA of all funds. As such, the U.S. figures likely underestimate the sector‚Äôs cross-fund holdings in that they do not include fund shareholdings of funds other than funds of funds. We therefore investigate (a) the economics behind the strong growth of cross-fund holdings and (b) their financial stability implications. While a large literature has investigated direct institution-to-institution connections and their financial stability implications in the context of banks (e.g.,Elliott, Golub, and Jackson 2014), this paper is the first to analyze such questions in the context of mutual funds. A major distinguishing feature between these two types of institutions is their liability structure: while banks are almost exclusively debt-financed, funds are predominantly equity-financed. Hence, cross-holdings between funds are in equity-like instruments, whereas banks‚Äô cross-holdings are mainly in debt instruments (e.g., interbank loans and debt instruments). Differences in both the payoff structure and the redemption mechanism of these instruments therefore give rise to different types of direct contagion. Intuitively, the underlying network of cross-fund connections could therefore serve as a means to transmit losses, which may amplify and redistribute fire sale vulnerabilities. For example, when a given fund A invests in another fund B, losses of fund B will also directly affect fund A. If other funds now hold shares of fund A, losses of fund B (and of fund A) propagate further through the cross-holdings network. Since fund net asset values (NAVs) are typically updated on a daily basis and generally reflect mark-to-market valuation changes, this contagion mechanism is relevant in good times and in bad.1Differences in the redemption mechanism also play a role: bank bonds are traded on secondary markets, but their sales do not directly affect banks‚Äô liability structure. In contrast, fund shares are directly exchanged against cash in the primary market, which immediately shortens a fund‚Äôs balance sheet and generates trading pressure. We study whether cross-fund holdings and cross-fund liquidations elevate vulnerabilities.

To this end, we draw on a unique data set for Germany, which hosts one of the largest fund markets in the euro area.2The data set includes highly granular information on funds‚Äô balance sheets, with detailed portfolio holdings at the monthly frequency across the most important asset classes (bonds, equities, and fund shares). Crucially, the data set covers both retail and institutional funds, allowing us to capture the largest part of the German fund sector‚Äôs total assets under management.

In the first part of the paper, we analyze the economics behind the strong growth of cross-fund holdings in Germany. The aggregate growth pattern inFigure 1is strongly driven by the category of mixed funds, in particular institutional ones, but retail funds also showed a marked increase in their cross-fund holdings. Moreover, there is a certain level of segmentation in that both retail and institutional funds‚Äô cross-holdings are predominantly with other retail and institutional funds, respectively.

We then turn to the question of why funds increasingly invest in other funds. Our analyses suggest that both diversification and liquidity management considerations play a role. Starting with diversification, we find that cross-fund investments increase funds‚Äô portfolio diversification (e.g., number of assets exposed to and portfolio HHI). However, these cross-fund investments increase exposure to more volatile, less liquid, and nondomestic asset markets compared to funds‚Äô direct reported asset holdings. In addition, we provide evidence that cross-fund holdings tend to make fund returns more volatile and in fact increase return procyclicality. Remarkably, these differences are particularly pronounced during periods with large changes in market uncertainty (as proxied by VIX changes). For example, for largeincreasesin the VIX, funds that invest in other funds generally display aworseperformance because of their fund investments. In line withIbragimov, Jaffee, and Walden (2011), we provide evidence that individual diversification efforts (through increased cross-fund investments) may come at the risk of systemic commonality. Specifically, we find that cross-fund holdings increase funds‚Äô effective portfolio overlap and lead to more homogeneous returns.

We then investigate whether fund shares are used for flow-driven liquidity management. Fund shares are reasonably liquid instruments, since investors in open-ended mutual funds can typically redeem their shares on any given day at the fund‚Äôs closing NAV. Hence, fund shares potentially could be more liquid than some of the underlying assets and therefore also could be used when fund managers themselves are forced to liquidate assets in the presence of investor redemptions. In line with this hypothesis, we show that, when faced with large outflows, a substantial share of funds‚Äô overall trading activity is indeed in fund shares. This suggests that fund managers use their cross-fund investments as part of their liquidity management strategy in response to (out)flows. Remarkably, we show that fund shares made up a substantial part of German funds‚Äô overall asset liquidations during the COVID-19-induced stress episode in March 2020, amounting to 39%(37%‚ÅÝ) of retail (institutional) funds‚Äô overall asset sales. Hence, fund managers indeed appear to value the liquidity transformation service offered by funds, particularly so during stress periods (Fricke, Jank, and Wilke 2022). In addition, followingFalato et al. (2020), we provide evidence of spillover effects across funds, where a given fund‚Äôs performance and flows are both sensitive to the flow pressure of its peer funds. Thus, cross-fund liquidations, similar to the liquidation of bonds and equities, have a measurable negative impact on fund performance and flows. Lastly, we provide evidence that equities and corporate bonds with higher exposure to cross-fund liquidation risk performed worse during March 2020. This suggests that the cross-fund dimension may also matter at the security level. Overall, our findings for the German fund sector indicate that cross-fund investments can be detrimental from a macroprudential perspective.

Building on these empirical observations, the second part of the paper explores the financial stability implications of both cross-fund investments and liquidations. For this purpose, we include both aspects in an otherwise ‚Äústandard‚Äù model of price-mediated contagion (e.g.,Greenwood, Landier, and Thesmar 2015;Fricke and Fricke 2021), which allows us to quantify their contribution to the fund sector‚Äôs vulnerability. The cross-fund dimension plays a role in several modeling steps: first, the initial shock will induce losses on funds‚Äô direct asset holdings (bonds and equities) which then propagate through the cross-fund holdings network, resulting in additional (delayed) portfolio losses. Second, we explicitly allow funds to liquidate fund shares under stress, taking into account that this selling pressure will increase the overall selling pressure of affected funds. In other words, to satisfy these additional cross-fund redemptions, affected funds will have to sell more of their direct asset holdings. These additional sales, and the resulting price pressure, represent another cross-fund-related component. Of course, fire sale losses on bonds and equities will again affect funds‚Äô NAVs via the cross-fund holdings network. Our framework then allows us to compare the overall contribution of the cross-fund components with the standard fire sale component. The sector‚Äôs aggregate vulnerability (AV) is the sum of funds‚Äô portfolio losses due to their common asset liquidations and their cross-fund holdings, relative to aggregate total net assets (TNA).

Our main finding from the model application is that the cross-fund components contribute substantially to the German fund sector‚Äôs AV, which suggests that previous work likely underestimated fund sector vulnerabilities. Specifically, imposing an initial shock corresponding to the market dynamics around the collapse of Lehman Brothers (amounting to initial losses of 2.4%of the sector‚Äôs TNA on average), we find that the relative contribution of the cross-fund components to the AV increased from 13.5%in September 2009 to 31.6%in June 2020. During the same period, the AV increased from 1.6%(or 61%of the initial shock) to more than 2%(or 89%of the initial shock). Strikingly, this increase in the AV is largely driven by the different cross-fund components, suggesting that the cross-fund dimension is indeed becoming increasingly important from a financial stability perspective. We also compare the estimated AVs to two null models which shut down all cross-fund-related channels. In both cases, we find that the actual AVs consistently exceed the null model AVs, adding further evidence that direct fund-to-fund connections tend to elevate fund sector vulnerabilities.

Our framework also yields two fund-level connectedness measures: systemicness (‚ÅÝS‚ÅÝ) quantifies how much of the aggregate losses are due to any given fund. Vulnerability (‚ÅÝV‚ÅÝ) quantifies how big the losses received by any given fund would be. In general, these two measures do not coincide, and we indeed find a relatively low correlation of 0.18. We provide evidence that these connectedness measures, in particular vulnerability, substantially improve the prediction of future realized fund fragility (e.g., return volatility and trading activity) over different forward horizons. Taken together, these findings highlight the potential usefulness of our methodology in terms of assessing fund sector vulnerabilities. We should note that our paper is accompanied by an extensiveInternet Appendix.

Our paper contributes to several streams of the literature. First and foremost, our paper adds to the literature on structural fund sector vulnerabilities (Coval and Stafford 2007;Chernenko and Sunderam 2020;Falato et al. 2020). This literature primarily focuses on fire sale vulnerabilities. From a theoretical perspective, cross-fund investments could potentially even reduce fund sector vulnerabilities if individual and aggregate diversification benefits would go hand in hand. Our empirical analysis of the German fund sector suggests that the increase in cross-fund investments has largely detrimental effects. This corroborates recent evidence that mutual funds ‚Äìas investors in other funds‚Äì may be an important driver of the fund flow externality (Fricke, Jank, and Wilke 2022).

Relatedly, while a rich literature is concerned with direct institution-to-institution contagion in interbank networks (e.g.,Elliott, Golub, and Jackson 2014;Acemoglu, Ozdaglar, and Tahbaz-Salehi 2015;Glasserman and Young 2016), we are not aware of similar work in the context of nonbank financial intermediaries. Specifically, this paper is the first to analyze the structure and dynamics of cross-fund holdings in the German fund sector, while previous work on cross-fund holdings largely focused on the within fund-family dimension.3

Lastly, our results point to the growing complexity of the asset management sector with important consequences for fragility. Hence, our paper adds to the literature on the relationship between complexity/uncertainty and systemic risk (Gai, Haldane, and Kapadia 2011;Caballero and Simsek 2013). For example,Caballero and Simsek (2013)present a model where financial crises arise endogenously due to banks‚Äô uncertainty about the network structure of cross-bank exposures. Without detailed knowledge on the network structure of exposures, banks face an increasingly complex environment since they need to understand a greater fraction of the financial network to assess their own financial health. Our paper is the first to raise a similar issue in the context of mutual funds.
We draw on a unique data set for the German investment fund sector, which is one of the largest fund markets in the euro area and has grown substantially since the global financial crisis (seeFigure IA.5inInternet Appendix D). The sector‚Äôs relative importance within the German financial system also continues to increase, as it made up 14%of the total assets of the German financial system in December 2019 (8%in December 2009).

Our data set consists of all open-ended investment funds, excluding ETFs, reporting to the investment fund statistics of the Deutsche Bundesbank.4We focus on the four dominant fund types, namely, equity funds, bond funds, mixed funds, and fund-of-funds (FoFs). As of December 2019, these fund types cover approx. 90%of the total assets under management of the German investment fund sector, with mixed funds (also referred to as allocation or balanced funds), which invest in a broad range of asset classes, being the dominant category. FollowingFalato et al. (2020), we use data on individual share classes, since investors‚Äô redemption decisions may depend on share class specific features. We complement the data with granular asset-level information from the Eurosystem‚Äôs Centralized Securities Database (CSDB).

The German investment fund sector consists of two different subsectors:Publikumsfonds(or retail funds) are akin to traditional open-ended retail funds which are open to both private and institutional investors.Spezialfonds(or institutional funds) are specialized investment vehicles for institutional investors, which are generally not available to regular retail investors. These institutional funds are typically tailored to the specific needs of a very small number (often just one) of large institutional investors, making run risks less relevant. As of December 2019, institutional funds make up roughly 79%of the German fund sector‚Äôs total assets under management. Notably, these institutional funds do not report to standard market databases (e.g., Morningstar).

Our final sample covers the period between September 2009 up until June 2020. This includes a total number of 10,804 unique funds, of which 1,639 are equity funds, 2,340 bond funds, 6,296 mixed funds, and 529 fund-of-funds. Sixty-three percent of our sample funds are institutional.Table 1provides key summary statistics for our sample funds. On average, institutional funds are twice as large as retail ones. In line with them being tailor-made for the specific needs of a small number of large institutional investors, they often display no flows whatsoever. Specifically, 82%(16%‚ÅÝ) of all fund-month observations for institutional (retail) funds have flows of exactly zero. Institutional funds tend to display both lower cash holdings and fund shareholdings as a percentage of their TNA.5While the median fund in our sample does not invest in other funds, the average portfolio share of fund holdings is close to 20%across the full sample. Notably, close to 50%of all fund-month observations (both for retail and institutional funds) contain nonzero fund holdings. Hence, conditional on investing in at least one other fund, the portfolio share of fund investments is indeed sizeable.

article.2. Growth and Economics of Cross-Fund Ho:
Figure 1provided the first evidence that cross-fund holdings in Germany increased dramatically over the last decade. This section dives deeper into these growth dynamics and their underlying reasons.

2.1 Structural features

Figure 2breaks down the share of funds‚Äô fund shareholdings by retail and institutional funds. Clearly, the dynamics for institutional funds are quantitatively very similar to the aggregate dynamics inFigure 1, with total fund holdings increasing from less than 10%in 2009 to more than 20%in 2020. While retail funds also increasingly invest in other funds, the growth pattern was somewhat weaker (from 11%in 2009 to 15%in 2020) and not entirely synchronized with the pattern of institutional funds, starting only in mid-2012. Remarkably, the growth of cross-fund investments is not driven by a strong increase in funds‚Äô holdings of money market funds (MMFs) or ETFs. This indicates that, when German funds invest in other funds, these investments are predominantly in traditional open-ended funds.

Figure 2 Cross-fund holdings in Germany over time The figure shows the aggregated portfolio share (relative to TNA) of fund shares in German investment funds‚Äô asset portfolios. The left (right) panel shows results for retail (institutional) funds. The sample period is September 2009 until June 2020. Open in new tab Download slide

To dive deeper into the structure of cross-fund holdings within the German fund sector,Figure 3shows a heatmap of the aggregate distribution of German fund shareholdings between different fund types in December 2019. Each cell shows how much funds in rowxhold of funds in columnyin their asset portfolios (in EUR bn). The left panel shows the results by fund type. Overall, mixed funds are by far the largest category that invests in other funds, with 54%(or 75.5 EU bn) of the overall cross-fund holdings being due to this fund type. Strikingly, around 58%(44 EUR bn) of mixed funds‚Äô cross-fund holdings are in other mixed funds, but mixed funds‚Äô investments in bond and equity funds are also sizable. In other words, the increasing prevalence of cross-fund holdings in Germany is not mechanically driven by the growth of dedicated fund-of-funds. Nevertheless, this fund category also plays a role, making up 40%(or 55.8 EUR bn) of the overall cross-fund holdings. Again, these funds heavily invest in mixed funds, but also show sizable investments in both equity and bond funds. The right panel ofFigure 3shows a similar decomposition by retail versus institutional funds. Clearly, cross-fund holdings are mainly due to institutional funds, with around 91%of the overall cross-fund holdings occurring within the institutional segment.6Remarkably, retail funds hardly invest in institutional funds, but concentrate their fund holdings almost exclusively on other retail funds.

2.2 Why do funds invest in other funds?

We now turn to the economic motivations behind the increasing tendency of funds to invest in other funds. Specifically, we look at portfolio diversification, and (flow-driven) liquidity management. We also touch on the effects of cross-fund holdings in terms of their impact on portfolio overlap.

2.2.1 Portfolio allocation: Diversification versus risk-taking

An important motivation to invest in other funds could be diversification considerations, and our first hypothesis is that funds use cross-fund holdings to diversify their asset portfolios. The economic motivation for this hypothesis is straightforward: mutual funds often provide their investors with a rather diversified asset portfolio. Moreover, by holding fund shares, funds can buy into specific assets or market segments without the need of building costly expertise. Compared with holding the underlying asset portfolio directly, fund investors can liquidate this portfolio straightforwardly by redeeming the corresponding fund shares. Prior evidence suggests that institutional investors in general, and investment funds in particular, tend to display a strong preference for mutual funds with relatively illiquid asset portfolios (Fricke, Jank, and Wilke 2022). We provide further evidence along these lines.

Portfolio structure. To assess whether portfolio diversification via cross-fund investments plays a role, we compare funds‚Äô reported asset portfolios with their asset portfolios after looking through their (German) fund share holdings, that is, ‚Äúreplacing‚Äù fund shareholdings with the underlying asset portfolios. Put simply, the idea is to compare funds‚Äô direct asset holdings (i.e., nonfund investments) with their implicit asset holdings after looking through their fund shareholdings. Technically speaking, the portfolio look-through involves taking into account the full network structure of funds‚Äô direct asset holdings and their cross-fund investments. (The technical details of our look-through procedure are relegated toInternet Appendix A.) At the fund-month level, we then compare funds‚Äô reported portfolios with their look-through portfolios. Of course, this distinction will only be relevant for funds that are part of the cross-fund holdings network, since otherwise the reported and look-through portfolios will be identical. Therefore, the following analyses are based on the subsample of funds that invest in at least one other fund in a given cross-section.

As before, we differentiate between retail and institutional funds. We focus on a variety of portfolio characteristics that directly relate to diversification, liquidity, and asset allocation. First, we compute two measures of funds‚Äô portfolio concentration, namely, the number of securities held and the standard Hirschman-Herfindahl index (HHI). Second, to assess the liquidity of the underlying assets, we compute funds‚Äô portfolioAmihud (2002)ratio.7Lastly, we decompose the portfolios by asset class and investment region. We winsorize the different measures at the 1%/99%levels.

Table 2shows the results. For each of the portfolio characteristics, we show the sample mean before (column 2) and after (column 3) the portfolio look-through, based on fund-month-level observations. We also report the difference between the two in column 4. Given that the characteristics inTable 2for the reported portfolios and the look-through portfolios are not sampled independently, we conduct pairedt-tests on the difference to assess statistical significance. (The results are robust to performing standardt-tests with bootstrapped standard errors.)

In line with our main hypothesis, we find that cross-fund investments increase portfolio diversification: most strikingly, funds gain (indirect) exposure to substantially more securities through their fund shareholdings. Specifically, retail funds increase their average security holdings from 69 (reported portfolio) to 202 (after look-through) unique securities. For institutional funds, the increase is even larger from 149 (reported portfolio) to 611 (after look-through). This result directly relates to the increasing importance of cross-fund holdings, as fund A, while holding shares of fund B, gains exposure to the securities directly held by fund B, but also gains an implicit exposure to the securities held by funds that are in the portfolio of fund B and so on. In line with an increased portfolio diversification, the look-through HHI is significantlylowerthan the HHI of the reported portfolio. The difference is also economically significant: for institutional (retail) funds, the look-through HHI is 56%(17%‚ÅÝ) lower than the reported HHI. This suggests that, compared with their direct portfolio holdings, funds gain exposure in different individual securities through their cross-fund investments. Moreover, the results on the portfolio Amihud suggest that these assets are less liquid compared to funds‚Äô direct investments. Hence, in line with the findings ofFricke, Jank, and Wilke (2022), funds indeed appear to value the liquidity transformation services offered by other funds.

Crucially,Table 2also reports the portfolio holdings by asset class and by investment region before and after the look-through. We find that fund holdings disproportionately increase funds‚Äô exposure to asset classes with high market risk (equities) or high liquidity risk (nonsovereign bonds). At the same time, funds tend to increase their exposure toward nondomestic markets, thereby reducing the home bias in their asset allocation. These differences are highly statistically significant and generally more pronounced for institutional funds: for example, the reported nonsovereign bond exposure of institutional (retail) funds is 32%(23%‚ÅÝ), but their actual exposure after the look-through is 38%(25%‚ÅÝ) on average. Overall, despite only focusing on cross-fund holdings within the German fund sector, the documented effects on fund portfolios are economically sizable.

Impact on fund returns. The patterns inTable 2are broadly in line with the hypothesis that funds diversify their asset portfolios through their cross-fund investments. However, since these investments increase funds‚Äô exposure in potentially more volatile, less liquid, and nondomestic asset markets, our second hypothesis is that cross-fund holdings could make fund returns more volatile and increase return procyclicality. In other words, more diversified asset portfolios could have an effective risk-taking effect. We provide evidence in line with this hypothesis.

For this purpose, we compare the distribution of funds‚Äô actual (i.e., realized) returns,R‚ÅÝ, and their cross-fund holdings adjusted returns,Radj.‚ÅÝ. As the term suggests, cross-fund holdings adjusted returns are funds‚Äô realized returns, adjusted for the effect of cross-fund holdings. We provide more technical details in Section3and only state the relevant equation here:

R = [ I N ‚àí E ‚àí 1 √ó a FUND ] ‚àí 1 R adj. , (1)

whereEis the diagonal matrix of fund TNAs,INis the identity matrix, andaFUNDis the cross-fund holdings network with each cellaf,gFUNDcorresponding to the amount of fundgin fundf‚Äôs portfolio. The above relationship makes clear that, through the cross-holdings network, fundf‚Äôs return may depend on all other funds‚Äô returns in the network.

Our data set provides us with information on all variables in Equation (1), exceptRadj.‚ÅÝ, which we can back out using the above relationship. SinceRandRadj.will coincide for funds without any fund holdings in their portfolios, we again focus on funds that are actually invested in other funds. Moreover, since fund-of-funds‚Äô asset portfolios almost exclusively consist of fund shares, theirRadj.will often be close to zero. We therefore focus on the three other fund types in our analysis, that is, equity, bond, and mixed funds, which makes it more difficult to find significant differences betweenRandRadj.‚ÅÝ.

Figure 4shows that, in line with our second hypothesis, fund returns are indeed more volatile after taking cross-fund investments into account. The top panel shows the two return distributions over the full sample,8where we find thatœÉ(R)is indeed 7%larger thanœÉ(Radj.)‚ÅÝ, as the distribution ofRhas more probability mass on moderate and large returns compared toRadj.‚ÅÝ.9Using paired sample t-tests, we find that the differences between funds‚Äô realized and cross-fund holdings adjusted returns are statistically significant at all standard confidence levels. (These results are robust to using Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests with bootstrapped standard errors.)

To further understand the source of these differences, the bottom panels ofFigure 4separate between fund-month observations where the change in the VIX was in the top/bottom quintile of the historical distribution. We find highly asymmetric patterns between these market states. For example, for largeincreasesin the VIX (bottom-left panel), funds that invest in other funds generally display aworseperformance due to their fund investments, with substantial probability mass on negative return differences. The reverse pattern can be observed for fund-month observations with a largedecreasein the VIX (bottom-right panel). Paired samplet-tests again indicate that these differences are statistically significant at all standard confidence levels.

Overall, in line with our second hypothesis, these results suggest that cross-fund holdings in fact increase the volatility and procyclicality of fund returns with positive and negative returns being amplified during extreme market states. As a consequence, while the results inTable 2suggest that diversification considerations appear to play a role, the actual diversification benefits may be less pronounced in extreme market states. The next analysis shows that the systemic diversification benefits could be even smaller.

Impact on portfolio overlap. We documented that individual funds‚Äô diversification efforts might have negative side effects as funds‚Äô realized returns become more procyclical, especially so during extreme market states. Another side effect of individual diversification could be an increased systemic asset commonality as inIbragimov, Jaffee, and Walden (2011). The idea is that, while individual funds gain exposure toward additional assets via their cross-fund holdings, it could be that other funds engaging in the same activity could increase exposure to the very same assets. Hence, our third hypothesis is that individual diversification via cross-fund investments effectively increases the portfolio overlap in bonds and equities.

To shed light on this, we investigate the portfolio overlap between funds in our sample, before and after looking through fund shares. FollowingAnt√≥n and Polk (2014), the overlap between fundsfandg‚ÅÝ, in a given cross-section, is defined as:

Overlap f , g = ‚àë H EuroHoldings f , H + EuroHoldings g , H TNA f + TNA g , (2)

whereHis the set of assets that fundsfandgare both invested in.

Figure 5shows the average portfolio overlap across all fund-pairs (again conditioning on funds that are invested in at least one other fund) at each point in time, both for funds‚Äô reported portfolios (in blue) and after the look-through (in red). In line with our third hypothesis, the average portfolio overlap is almost 4 timeslargerafter looking through fund shares. In other words, after looking through cross-fund holdings, funds‚Äô asset portfolios are much more similar than suggested by their reported portfolios. Notably, the average portfolio overlap based on the reported holdings remained rather stable from late 2011 onward, whereas the look-through portfolio overlap showed more variation. Thus, even though individual funds may aim to diversify their asset portfolios by means of their fund shareholdings, this behavior actually increases the commonality of funds‚Äô asset portfolios. These findings suggest that individual diversification may increase systemic commonality as inIbragimov, Jaffee, and Walden (2011).10

2.2.2 Flow-driven liquidity management

In addition to offering potential diversification benefits, fund shares can be considered reasonably liquid instruments and could therefore be relevant for funds‚Äô liquidity management (e.g., to satisfy investor redemptions). This is because investors in open-ended mutual funds can typically redeem their shares on any given day at the fund‚Äôs closing NAV. Hence, by means of this liquidity transformation, fund shares could potentially be more liquid than some of the underlying assets. We therefore hypothesize that fund shares should be particularly relevant when fund managers themselves are forced to liquidate assets in the presence of investor redemptions. (Note that this formulation implicitly opens up a network perspective on fund flows. We return to this aspect in Section3.)

In this regard, it should be kept in mind that retail funds‚Äô flows are generally more volatile than those of institutional funds (Table 1) and that retail funds face outflows much more often then institutional funds. Therefore, retail fund managers may be relatively more inclined to make use of their fund investments for liquidity management purposes.11To acknowledge potential structural differences, we again differentiate between institutional and retail funds.

Table 3provides evidence in line with our hypothesis, showing the TNA-weighted average portfolio adjustments across different asset classes as a function of the magnitude of fund outflows (by quintile). As such, we condition on fund-month observations where funds experience outflows. 2.3 The economic relevance of cross-fund liquidations

Having established that fund shares may be used to accommodate outflows, we wish to dive deeper into the economic relevance of cross-fund liquidations. For this purpose, we first take a closer look at the trading patterns of German investment funds during the first half of 2020. Afterward, we estimate a generalized version ofFalato et al. (2020)to quantify the impact of peer funds‚Äô asset liquidations across the main asset classes in our sample. We also provide indicative evidence for the COVID-19 episode that the cross-fund dimension also matters at the security level. These analyses will set the stage for our study of the financial stability implications of the cross-fund dimension in Section3.

2.3.1 Cross-fund holdings during market stress

We now turn toward a more dynamic view on how German funds treat their fund shareholdings during market stress, in particular during March 2020, that is, the month when the COVID-19-induced price shock rattled global financial markets (Falato, Goldstein, and Horta√ßsu 2021).Figure 6shows the aggregate trading volumes (in EUR billion) of retail and institutional funds between January and May 2020. As in the previous subsection, we show the aggregate active trading component (evaluated at prices int‚àí1‚ÅÝ), broken down by asset class. For the sake of reference, the red line (corresponding to the right-handy-axis) shows the time-series of the VIX during the same period, highlighting the large increase in market uncertainty/volatility in March 2020. Clearly, both retail and institutional funds displayed large asset liquidations across all asset classes in March 2020, amounting to 17 and 23 EUR bn (or 2.6%and 1.4%of their total asset portfolios), respectively. In line with the analyses in the previous subsection, fund shares made up a substantial part of these asset liquidations, amounting to 39%(37%‚ÅÝ) of retail (institutional) funds‚Äô overall asset sales. Note that these contributions are substantially larger than the aggregate portfolio weights of fund shares in funds‚Äô asset portfolios (Figure 2), such that fund shares were liquidated overproportionally in the aggregate. As noted previously, this could be because fund shares are rather liquid: through the daily redemption possibility, redeeming fund shares remained a viable option during March 2020, whereas certain market segments (corporate bonds in particular) effectively disappeared.12

Figure 6also documents substantial differences between retail and institutional funds. Strikingly, retail funds faced aggregate outflows of 8 EUR bn (or‚àí2%of their TNA) in March 2020. On the other hand, institutional funds received aggregateinflowsof 10 EUR bn (or +0.6%of their TNA) in March and showed outflows of 5.2 EUR bn only in April. This suggests that the sizable asset liquidations of institutional funds in March may have been motivated by factors other than investor flows. We confirm that this is indeed the case by followingShek, Shim, and Shin (2018), where we split funds‚Äô overall trading activity into a flow-driven and a discretionary component. Specifically, the taxonomy builds on the comparison between fund flows and changes in cash holdings (seeTable IA.3inInternet Appendix D). The idea is that, for example, if a fund faced moderateoutflowsand anincreasein cash holdings during the same period, the fund manager likely engaged in discretionary trading.13Based on this taxonomy, we calculated the two components at the individual fund-level and then aggregated these over all funds in the relevant subsample. The aggregate statistics inFigure IA.7inInternet Appendix Dsuggest that around two-thirds of retail funds‚Äô overall trading activity in March 2020 was flow-driven and only one-third was discretionary (or non-flow-driven). In contrast, institutional funds‚Äô liquidations were almost exclusively discretionary. Consistent withMorris, Shim, and Shin (2017), uncertainty shocks (such as COVID-19), can go along with substantial discretionary sales by institutional fund managers.

Overall, these results highlight that institutional funds can still display highly procyclical trading behavior despite the apparent absence of flow pressure (Barbu, Fricke, and Moench 2020). We acknowledge these behavioral patterns in our assessment of the financial stability implications of cross-fund holdings in Section3.

2.3.2 Direct evidence on cross-fund contagion

We now wish to provide direct evidence in support of cross-fund induced contagion in our data set. Specifically, followingFalato et al. (2020), we provide evidence of spillover effects across funds, where a given fund‚Äôs performance and flows are both sensitive to the price pressure of its peer funds that hold the same securities. As noted byFalato et al. (2020), cross-correlations in the performance (and flows) of peer funds are a major reduced-form implication of fire sale spillovers.

Specifically, whileFalato et al. (2020)focus on corporate bonds, we estimate a more generalized specification that incorporates trading pressure across the three major asset classesc(bonds, equities, and fund shares):

y t , f = Œ≤ 0 + ‚àë c Œ≤ c √ó PeerFlowPressure t , f c + Œ≥ √ó X t ‚àí 1 , f + œµ t , f , (5)

where the dependent variables are either fund returns or fund flows. The main explanatory variables are the threePeerFlowPressurevariables, which are the fund-specific portfolio-weighted price pressure from other funds‚Äô flow-related trading in a given asset classc‚ÅÝ, constructed as inFalato et al. (2020). The corresponding asset-level price pressure variables are constructed, separately for the three main asset classes, as the relative proportion of flow-induced buys minus flow-induced sales of peer funds (which occur whenever a fund displays flows in the top/bottom decile of fund flows). These pressure variables exclude fundfs own trading activity to avoid mechanical correlation. The PeerFlowPressure variables quantify how exposed a given fund‚Äôs portfolio is to the fire sales of other funds in a given asset class. We focus on funds that invest in at least one other fund. We standardize the three PeerFlowPressure variables (zero mean, unit standard deviation). Controls include lagged fund TNA, family TNA, flows, and returns. All regressions include fund and time FEs. Standard errors are clustered by fund and date.

Panel A ofTable 5shows the results with fund performance (returns, columns 1 to 3) and flows (columns 4 to 6) as the outcome variables. The coefficient on PeerFlowPressure is positive and highly statistically significant for fund returns across all specifications. The results for fund flows are comparable, except for a lack of significance of the bond and equity PeerFlowPressure variables in the case of institutional funds. Crucially, in line with the fire sale mechanism, cross-fund liquidations from peer funds harm fund performance and flows in all specifications. In panel B, we separate the flow pressure variables into their buys and sales components, finding a certain level of asymmetry in the strength of the relationship. Overall, these results indicate that fund share liquidations, similar to the liquidation of direct asset holdings of bonds and equities, have a measurable negative impact on fund performance and fund flows. Hence, cross-fund liquidations are both statistically and economically relevant.

2.3.3 Security-level effects during March 2020

The main channel we have in mind is that cross-fund connections affect fund-level outcomes and we provided evidence that cross-fund investments and liquidations indeed have a measurable impact on fund performance (and flows). We now turn to the question of whether there are measurable security-level effects as well. The empirical identification of such a cross-fund induced security-level fragility is extremely challenging as it would require isolating cross-fund flow-induced trading from the standard flow-induced trading channel. In the absence of a clear idiosyncratic shock on parts of the cross-fund network and given a lack of high-frequency portfolio information, tracing the sequence of cross-fund liquidations in a clean way is not straightforward.

With these caveats in mind, we now provide indicative evidence that securities with higher ownership by cross-held funds performed worse during the COVID-19-induced stress episode. Our focus on this particular episode is motivated by the empirical observation inFig. 6that fund shares were heavily liquidated during March 2020. We hypothesize that the ultimate security-level effect of such cross-fund liquidations should be particularly measurable for securities that are held by funds which themselves are strongly held by other funds.14We therefore run the following regression for the period between January 2020 and May 2020:

article.3. Fund Sector Vulnerabilities and the C:
Having established that cross-fund investments are (a) becoming increasingly important over time, (b) were heavily liquidated during March 2020, and (c) display measurable contagion effects, we propose a general framework to assess the contribution of cross-fund holdings to the fund sector‚Äôs vulnerability. To this end, we explicitly include direct cross-fund connections in a standard model of price-mediated contagion (e.g.,Greenwood, Landier, and Thesmar 2015;Fricke and Fricke 2021). In brief, the modeling steps are as follows:

Step 0:Initial shock on prices of funds‚Äô direct asset holdings (bonds and equities).Step 1:The mark-to-market losses from step 0 propagate through the cross-fund holdings network and induce additional losses.Step 2:Fund managers liquidate assets (bonds, equitiesandfund shares) due to(a)Flow-driven trading (flow-performance relationship, FPR).(b)Discretionary trading (e.g., cash hoarding).Step 3:These asset liquidations negatively affect market prices:(a)Losses on funds‚Äô bond and equity holdings.(b)Additional losses due to cross-fund holdings.

Our main modeling contribution is to incorporate losses due to cross-fund holdings (steps 1 and 3b) and to allow for fund share liquidations (step 2). The fund sector‚Äôs aggregate vulnerability (AV) is defined as the sum of funds‚Äô portfolio losses due to their common asset liquidations and their cross-fund holdings, relative to the aggregate preshock TNA.

Step 0: Initial shock

The initial shockFMKTon market prices leads to immediate mark-to-market portfolio losses due on funds‚Äô direct bond and equity holdings, whereas fund shareholdings will not immediately reflect these losses. Step 1: Impact of initial shock via funds‚Äô cross-holdings

The initial shock led to immediate mark-to-market losses on funds‚Äô direct asset holdings. Because of the different trading mechanism of fund shares, the initial shock affects fund share values with some delay as the initial portfolio losses enter into funds‚Äô updated NAV calculations and thus propagate through the cross-fund holdings network. For fund managers, to keep track of this would be a difficult (if not impossible) task, since they lack full information on funds‚Äô direct asset holdings and the structure of the cross-fund holdings network (e.g.,Caballero and Simsek 2013). In contrast, we know funds‚Äô exact portfolio composition and we can update the market value of funds‚Äô cross-holdings.

Intuitively, whenever a given fund invests in other funds, we need to take these funds‚Äô losses from step 0 into account. This involves an iterative process, where the posted losses from step 0 will allow fund managers to update their NAVs based on other funds‚Äô NAVs. Of course, incorporating losses on the cross-fund holdings will again negatively affect their own NAV. Hence, the process continues until convergence. It is straightforward to provide a closed-form solution based on the cross-holdings matrixaFUND‚ÅÝ. The total value of a given fundf‚Äôs fund holdings (after taking cross-fund holdings-related shock propagation into account) is simplyA1,fFUND=‚àëgaf,gFUND√ó(1+R1,gE)‚ÅÝ. 3.2 Measures of aggregate and fund-level vulnerability

The total nominal loss due to the initial shockFMKTis given by1N‚Ä≤AR0A‚ÅÝ. Using Equations (8), (15), and (16), we define the aggregate vulnerability of the fund sector as follows:

A V = 1 N ‚Ä≤ ( Loss 3 a Sales:Direct ‚èû Bonds and equities + Loss 1 Cross-fund + Loss 3 a Sales:Cross-fund + Loss 3 b Cross-fund ‚èû Cross-fund holdings ) ‚àë f E f . (17)

For the sake of comparison, we normalize overall losses by the sector‚Äôs TNA before the initial shock (‚ÅÝ‚àëfEf‚ÅÝ). Within our framework, these losses can be straightforwardly decomposed into losses due to funds‚Äô common holdings of bonds and stocks and losses due to cross-fund holdings, where the latter can be further broken down into losses from step 1 (‚ÅÝLoss1Cross-fund‚ÅÝ) and step 3 (‚ÅÝLoss3aSales:Cross-fund+Loss3bCross-fund‚ÅÝ).21We should note that previous work exclusively focused on losses due to (fire sales of) commonly held securities in step 3a (‚ÅÝLoss3aSales:Direct‚ÅÝ). The AV quantifies the vulnerability of the fund sector to contagion effects that stem from different layers of connectedness, both via common asset holdings (bonds and equities) and via direct fund-to-fund connections. The AV measures the percentage of aggregate TNA that would be wiped out following a given initial shock. Note that the AV is agnostic toward the nature of the underlying shock scenario. In line with prior work, we assume a negative shock scenario to quantify fund sector vulnerabilities. Note that if we were to impose a positive (rather than a negative) shock scenario instead, the cross-fund components would signal potential benefits from these channels.

We also decompose the AV into each fund‚Äôs individual contribution as inGreenwood, Landier, and Thesmar (2015)andDuarte and Eisenbach (2021). Our fund-level measure of systemicness (‚ÅÝSf‚ÅÝ) should capture a given fund‚Äôs contribution to the AV as in Equation (17):

S f = ( S f Sales:Direct + S f Cross-fund ) with A V = ‚àë f N S f . (18)

The first component of Equation(18) can be written as

S f Sales:Direct = 1 N ‚Ä≤ √ó A MKT √ó M ~ 1 √ó L √ó œï f , 1 : K ‚àë f E f ,

which are simply the losses stemming from fundf‚Äôs direct asset sales (withœïf,1:Kbeing the fund-specific asset sales, calculated separately for each fundfaccording to Equation (11)). The second component in Equation (18) is proportional to a fund‚Äôs network position:

S f Cross-fund = C f √ó ( 1 N ‚Ä≤ √ó Loss Cross-Fund ) ‚àë f E f ,

whereCf=(œâ‚àí1√ó1N‚àí1)‚ÅÝ, is the Katz network centrality, withœâdefined in Equation (7).Cfis normalized such that‚àëfCf=1andCf=0for funds without cross-fund holdings.

Lastly, we also define fund-level measures of vulnerability based on the different loss components. In particular, fundf‚Äôs vulnerability is simply

V f = Loss f Sales:Direct + Loss f Cross-fund E f = V f Sales:Direct + V f Cross-fund , (19)

whereVfSales:DirectandVfCross-fundare the contributions of the two broad loss components relative to the fund-specific TNAs. The two fund-level indicatorsSandVcapture different aspects of a given fund‚Äôs connectedness. Specifically,Vfquantifies how much of the overall (hypothetical) losses are borne by fundf‚ÅÝ, whereasSfquantifies how much of these losses are due to fundf‚ÅÝ. In general, these two measures will not coincide.

3.3 (Cross-fund) vulnerabilities in the German fund sector

In the following, we apply our framework to the sample of German investment funds over the period between September 2009 and June 2020. Before presenting the main results, we briefly explain how we calibrated the key model parameters. We should highlight that, while our framework can accomodate a broad range of shock scenarios, motivated by the empirical observations in Section2and following previous work, we focus on a systematic shock (i.e., crisis times). We acknowledge that the qualitative results could potentially differ for alternative idiosyncratic shock scenarios.

Our modeling framework contains several behavioral parameters and a stress scenario.Table 7provides a concise overview of the specific parameter values used in the actual application and here we briefly describe our choice of the baseline parameter values. Moreover, we conduct extensive parameter sensitivity analyses, and our main results remain qualitatively unaffected across a wide parameter range around these baseline values. Details on parameter choice and sensitivity can be found inInternet Appendix C.

3.3.2 Results: Aggregate vulnerability and the cross-fund components

In what follows, we apply our framework separately for each month during the period between September 2009 and June 2020. Thus, for each month in our sample, we impose the same initial shock on all bonds and equities (‚ÅÝ‚àí8.4%and‚àí1.6%‚ÅÝ, respectively) and a VIX increase of+37.5%‚ÅÝ. On average, the initial shock in step 0 amounts to losses of 2.4%of the sector‚Äôs aggregate TNA.

Main results. The two panels ofFigure 7show different breakdowns of the AV based on the summation of the different fund-level systemicness components, as in Equation (18). On average, the AV amounts to losses of 71%of the initial shock (ranging between 61%and 87%‚ÅÝ) during our sample period. Hence, the German fund sector may indeed amplify stress in an adverse market environment. Regarding the level, we find that the AV amounts to 1.7%on average and we find a marked increase over time (from 1.6%in 2009 to more than 2.0%in 2020). Remarkably, this increase is largely driven by the cross-fund components as shown in the left panel: the dark blue area shows that the absolute contribution of fire sales of funds‚Äô direct bond and equity holdings remained stable at around 1.3 percentage points, while the cross-fund holdings-related components increased substantially over time. In fact, the absolute AV contribution of these components increased by a factor of three over our sample period (from 0.2 to 0.6 percentage points). The red line, corresponding to the right-handy-axis, indicates that the relative AV contribution of the cross-fund components increased from 13.5%in September 2009 to 31.6%in June 2020. These numbers show that cross-fund holdings-related losses display an even larger contribution to the overall AV than their aggregate portfolio share would suggest (Figure 1). Extensive sensitivity analyses inInternet Appendix Cshow that this finding is robust to variations in the main model parameters.

To put the relevance of cross-fund liquidations in perspective, we can also compare the relative composition of the two sale components. While direct asset sales (in blue) remain the dominant channel, cross-fund liquidations (in green) are becoming more relevant over time with an increase from 4.5%in September 2009 to 11.3%in June 2020.22

The right panel ofFigure 7decomposes the AV by fund type and by contagion channel. Both equity and bond funds contribute to the AV almost exclusively via their fire sales of direct asset holdings, consistent with the finding that fund shareholdings are less prevalent for these two fund types. In contrast, fund-of-funds contribute almost exclusively via the cross-holdings component, consistent with the fact that these fund hold hardly any direct investments in bonds and equities. Mixed funds contribute to the AV via both channels due to their sizeable investments in bonds, equities, and fund shares. Interestingly, mixed funds‚Äô AV contribution via direct asset sales remains rather stable over time, while the importance of their cross-fund components strongly increases over time (making up one-third of their overall AV contribution at the end of our sample).23

Null model AVs. Having established that the different cross-fund components are indeed an important driver of the AV, we now wish to understand how the AV would look like in a world without any cross-fund investments. Put simply, the idea is to replace cross-fund investments with direct asset holdings in bonds and equities to shut down all cross-fund-related channels. Hence, we need to choose adequate null models that tell us how funds‚Äô direct asset portfolios (marketable assets) would look like in such a world. To allow for a wide range of alternatives, we analyze two extreme cases: the first alternative,AV Look-through, is based on applying our modeling framework to the look-through portfolios from Section2. This approach assumes that, in a world without any cross-fund investments, funds‚Äô direct asset holdings in marketable assets would be as diversified as they are when investing through other funds. The second alternative,AV Direct, assumes that, in a world without cross-fund investments, funds would prefer to invest only in their reported direct bond/equity holdings. In this case, we scale up a given fund‚Äôs asset-level investments proportional to its fund shareholdings. We then apply the same methodology to these alternatives.

Figure 8shows that the actual AV consistently exceeds the two null model AVs by around 16%‚ÅÝ. Hence, fund sector vulnerabilities would be lower in a world without any cross-fund investments (and liquidations). The intuition behind this result is as follows: broadly speaking, any differences between the actual AV and the null model AVs could be driven by (a) differences in the numerator and/or (b) differences in the denominator. Regarding (b), the aggregate null model TNA will be smaller compared to the actual values (see, e.g.,Figure IA.1inInternet Appendix Afor the look-through case), which mechanically pushes the null model AVs upward. At the same time, the null model numerator consists of the very first component in Equation (17) only, that is, losses from direct asset sales, since all cross-fund-related channels are shut down. Depending on the magnitude and structure of funds‚Äô direct asset sales, the null model numerator could be smaller or larger than the actual numerator. The results inFigure 8indicate that the null model numerators are in fact substantiallylower. In fact, the differences would be even more pronounced if we were to take into account the simple size effect (b). To ensure that the differences inFigure 8are not mechanically driven by differences in the initial shock, we checked that the overall step 3 losses of the actual AV exceed those of the two null models in absolute terms (by roughly 3%‚ÅÝ), mainly because aggregate asset sales would be lower in the latter two cases. Overall, this analysis provides further evidence that the novel layer of direct fund-to-fund connections can elevate fund sector vulnerabilities.

3.3.3 Results: Fund-level fragility

The results presented so far were concerned with the sector‚Äôs AV. From a macroprudential perspective, it is crucial to identify funds that (a) are systemically important in that they contribute substantially to the AV and/or (b) funds that are particularly vulnerable to the actions of other funds and would bear large losses. We capture these two dimensions with our two fund-level indicators of systemicness(Sf)and vulnerability(Vf)‚ÅÝ. In line with our remarks at the end of Section3.1, the two measures indeed do not coincide in general and display a correlation betweenVandSof only 0.18 when pooling across all fund-month observations.24

We wish to assess the capacity of these fund-level indicators to predict individual funds‚Äô future realized fragility. Similar toGreenwood and Thesmar (2011)andDuarte and Eisenbach (2021), we run the following predictive panel regression:

y t + œÑ , f = Œ± + Œ≤ 1 √ó V t , f + Œ≤ 2 √ó S t , f + Œ≥ √ó X t , f + Œ± f + Œ± t + œµ t + œÑ , f , (22)

whereyt+œÑ,fis a measure of the fragility of fundfat timet+œÑandŒ±fandŒ±tare fund and time FEs. We hypothesize thatVis more relevant in this specific application in that it quantifies how large the hypothetical losses received by fundfwould be at any given point in time. In contrast,Sshould be less relevant for predicting a given fund‚Äôs own future fragility as it measures that fund‚Äôs hypothetical impact on other funds. As fragility measures we use (a) raw returns, (b) absolute returns as a proxy for return volatility (e.g.,Ang et al. 2006), and (c) trading activity as measured by%ŒîHoldings aggregated across all assetsi(Equation (3)). As controls, we include lagged fund TNA and leverage, as well as contemporaneous values of the respective fragility measure,yt,f‚ÅÝ. We let the forecast horizonœÑrange from 1 to 12 months. Standard errors are clustered by fund and date. For ease of interpretation, all variables entering the regression are standardized (zero mean, unit standard deviation).

Table 8shows the results. In line with our hypothesis, we find thatVis generally more relevant for predicting a given fund‚Äôs future realized fragility. With regard to fund returns (panel A), while the coefficients forVare generally insignificant these tend to be negative. In panel B we again look at fund returns but now estimate the relationship based on panel quantile regressions to model the conditional bottom quartile of the return distribution rather than the conditional mean. (To accurately estimate the panel quantile regression, we need to drop time FEs from the model specification.) Here, we find thatVis indeed significantly negative related to fund returns. This suggests that the relationship with fund fragility indeed differs substantially across the distribution of fund returns and thatVbetter captures individual funds‚Äô downside risk. In contrast, the coefficient forSis generally positive and often also significant. For return volatility we again find that the coefficient forVis positive across all horizons and significant in many cases. Remarkably, we find no such pattern forS‚ÅÝ.

article.4. Conclusion:
This paper establishes the empirical fact that investment funds increasingly invest in other funds. In the first part of the paper, we analyzed the economics behind the strong growth of such cross-fund holdings. In the second part of the paper, we quantified the implications of both cross-fund holdings and liquidations by adding this layer of connectedness to a standard model of price-mediated contagion. Our main conclusion is that the cross-fund dimension must be taken into account to accurately assess fund sector vulnerabilities. In fact, our analysis suggests that exclusively focusing on funds‚Äô common asset holdings could substantially underestimate fund sector vulnerabilities.

Looking forward, we believe that cross-fund investments deserve further study in other contexts. This is because, from a theoretical perspective, cross-fund investments could potentially even reduce fund sector vulnerabilities. While our analysis for the German fund sector highlights that the increasing trend of funds to invest in other funds has largely detrimental effects, alternative conclusions could emerge in different contexts.} Moreover, the observation that fund shares make up an increasing share of funds‚Äô portfolios could be seen as funds‚Äô asset portfolios becoming increasingly complex. This higher complexity, in and of itself, could make the sector more fragile (e.g.,Gai, Haldane, and Kapadia 2011;Caballero and Simsek 2013). Lastly, a challenging question is to what extent (cross-)fund vulnerabilities affect asset prices and, ultimately, the real economy. While a small literature on the real effects of fire sales has emerged (e.g.,Edmans, Goldstein, and Jiang 2012), we believe that more work along these lines is needed.
